description:
You are a monitoring specialist who has just inherited a half-finished configuration from another team.  
    Your goal is to finish configuring the alerting system and to leave a short audit trail of the exact
    changes you make.

    1. Directory layout that already exists when you start  
       • /home/user/monitoring/alerts.yaml  
       • /home/user/monitoring/notifications.toml  

       Nothing else is present inside /home/user/monitoring.  
       Do not create any new sub-directories; restrict yourself to the files listed above and to one
       additional log file (see step 3).

    2. Required final state of /home/user/monitoring/alerts.yaml  
       The YAML document must contain exactly three alert entries, in the order given below.  
       Make sure the indentation is two spaces and **never use tab characters**.  
       ```yaml
       alerts:
         - name: cpu_high
           threshold: 75
           enabled: true
         - name: memory_high
           threshold: 80
           enabled: true
         - name: disk_usage
           threshold: 85
           enabled: true
       ```

    3. Required final state of /home/user/monitoring/notifications.toml  
       The TOML document must look exactly like this, including quotation marks and spacing:  
       ```toml
       [smtp]
       enabled = true
       server  = "smtp.example.com"
       port    = 587
       sender  = "monitor@example.com"
       recipients = ["admin@example.com", "ops@example.com"]
       ```

    4. Create a change log  
       File path: /home/user/monitoring/change_log.txt  
       Permissions: leave default (0664 or more restrictive).  
       Log format requirements (one change per line, no header row):  
       • Column 1 – an ISO-8601 timestamp with timezone offset, e.g. 2025-02-17T14:05:23+00:00  
       • Column 2 – the literal filename (alerts.yaml or notifications.toml)  
       • Column 3 – a dotted “setting” path that pinpoints what changed, e.g. alerts[0].threshold  
       • Column 4 – the string “->”  
       • Column 5 – the value *before* the change  
       • Column 6 – the value *after* the change  
       Separate columns with a single space character.  
       Example line (do **not** copy verbatim):  
       2025-02-17T14:05:23+00:00 notifications.toml smtp.enabled false -> true

       Your log must contain **exactly five** lines in the following order:  
       1. alerts.yaml alerts[0].threshold …  
       2. alerts.yaml alerts[0].enabled …  
       3. alerts.yaml alerts[1].threshold …  
       4. alerts.yaml alerts[1].enabled …  
       5. notifications.toml smtp.enabled …

       Use the real old and new values that you actually touched, and capture the correct timestamp for
       each entry at the moment you perform the change.

    5. Hints & constraints  
       • You will only need roughly 3–4 shell commands in total (use any editor you like, or echo/printf, etc.).  
       • Do not hard-code the timestamp format incorrectly; the automated test will validate strict ISO-8601.  
       • Avoid leaving trailing whitespace in any of the configuration files.  
       • Do **not** add extra keys, comments or blank lines that are not described above.

    When you are done you should have the two configuration files in the exact state shown and a
    change_log.txt file that meets the formatting rules.  
    The automated test suite will fail on any deviation (wrong spacing, ordering, etc.), so double-check!
truth:
Initial files before the agent starts:
    1. /home/user/monitoring/alerts.yaml
       Contents (using LF endings):
       alerts:
         - name: cpu_high
           threshold: 85
           enabled: false
         - name: memory_high
           threshold: 90
           enabled: false

    2. /home/user/monitoring/notifications.toml
       Contents:
       [smtp]
       enabled = false
       server  = "smtp.example.com"
       port    = 587
       sender  = "monitor@example.com"
       recipients = ["admin@example.com"]

    Expected final files:

    1. /home/user/monitoring/alerts.yaml
       alerts:
         - name: cpu_high
           threshold: 75
           enabled: true
         - name: memory_high
           threshold: 80
           enabled: true
         - name: disk_usage
           threshold: 85
           enabled: true

    2. /home/user/monitoring/notifications.toml
       [smtp]
       enabled = true
       server  = "smtp.example.com"
       port    = 587
       sender  = "monitor@example.com"
       recipients = ["admin@example.com", "ops@example.com"]

    3. /home/user/monitoring/change_log.txt
       • Exactly 5 lines, LF line endings, no trailing blank line.  
       • First four lines must correspond to the two threshold and two enabled changes in alerts.yaml.  
         They must use the dotted notation alerts[0].threshold, alerts[0].enabled, etc., with the correct
         old and new values (85 -> 75, false -> true, 90 -> 80, false -> true).  
       • Fifth line records smtp.enabled false -> true.  
       • Timestamp column must match regex:
         ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}(?:\+|\-)[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}\s
       • Single space between every column, exactly one “->” token.

    File/directory permissions need not be modified; default umask is sufficient.
name: task_000000_11b5adfc
